items.q: What is an iterative design process?
items.correct: A cyclical process where designs are repeatedly tested, evaluated, and refined.
items.misconception: A linear process from brief to final product.
items.random_wrong: A process used only for software.
items.why: Students learn a 'step 1, step 2' sequence and apply it to iteration too. Iteration loops back - you might redesign after testing, then test again, many times.

items.q: What does an LDR's resistance do as light intensity INCREASES?
items.why: Students assume 'more input = more resistance', but LDR resistance is INVERSELY proportional to light. Dark = high resistance; bright = low resistance. This is why LDRs detect darkness in circuits.

items.q: If voltage is doubled and resistance is unchanged, what happens to the current?
items.correct: Current doubles (V = IR, so I = V/R).
items.why: Students get the direction of proportionality wrong. Current is PROPORTIONAL to voltage (when R is constant) - more push, more flow. They sometimes confuse this with current being inversely proportional to resistance.

items.q: Which block represents a sensor in a system diagram?
items.why: Correct answer: Input. “Process” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.
The answer examiners want is: Ohms. “Volts” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: An LED drops 2 V from a 6 V supply, taking 20 mA. What resistor value (ohms) is needed?
items.why: Exact answer: 200. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
